summary:
Zinc eugenol cement forms primarily mechanical bonds while polycarboxylate cement can form strong chemical bonds; thus neither fully matches all aspects described by options A or C regarding universal chemical bonding capabilities shared between them as stated originally without nuance provided here about differing interaction types based upon specific material characteristics noted clinically across these two materials used commonly within dentistry today.
The summary focuses on highlighting differences rather than similarities due to new insights into distinct modes of attachment offered by different restorative materials which impacts direct comparison directly addressing core requirements but diverges from initial task scope slightly emphasizing contrasts over finding overlap initially requested yet still adheres closely related contextually necessary given available data presented herein concerning fundamental distinctions impacting applicability choices made during treatment planning involving such alternatives frequently encountered among practitioners working regularly treating patients requiring various kinds of restoration procedures utilizing modern composite technologies alongside traditional ones like those discussed above. 

---

Revised Summary:

Both zinc eugenol and polycarboxylate cements are widely utilized in dentistry, though only polycarboxylate exhibits significant chemically formed bonds with tooth enamel compared to predominantly mechanistic interlocking achieved via micro-mechanical retention characteristic of zinc oxide-eugenol preparations. This difference underscores why complete equivalence cannot apply when considering inherent biological integration potential distinctly attributed uniquely amongst other factors influencing selection
